title: Positivity of the effective range for finite range attractive potentials with a repulsive core
abstract: In the phenomenological study of exotic hadrons, the sign of the effective range, $r_0$, is invoked as a criterion to distinguish between compact multiquark configurations (associated with $r_0 < 0$) and loosely bound hadronic molecules ($r_0 > 0$). Motivated by this, we investigate the fundamental constraints on the sign of the effective range for single-channel local interactions. We rigorously prove that for finite-range potentials, characterized by an inner repulsive core and an outer attractive tail, the effective range remains strictly positive provided that the scattering length is greater than the range of the potential ($a > R$).
summary: This paper proves that adding a repulsive core to an attractive finite-range potential cannot produce a negative effective range if the scattering length is larger than the potential range. This result implies that the negative effective ranges used to identify compact exotic hadrons cannot be explained by simple single-channel local potentials and instead require more complex dynamics like coupled-channels.
main_problem: The paper investigates whether a single-channel local potential with an inner repulsive core and an outer attractive tail can produce a negative effective range ($r_0 < 0$).
main_result: The author rigorously proves that for such finite-range potentials, the effective range remains strictly positive provided the scattering length is greater than the potential range ($a > R$).
method: The study uses the radial Schrödinger equation, effective range expansion (ERE), and an integral representation of the phase shift to derive a mathematical proof based on the convexity of the wave function.
model_or_system: The system consists of single-channel local, spherically symmetric, finite-range potentials, specifically modeled using a piecewise continuous spherical barrier/well and a truncated superposition of Yukawa potentials.
key_observables: Scattering length ($a$), effective range ($r_0$), and phase shift ($\delta_0$).
important_parameters: Potential range ($R$), repulsive core radius ($r_1$), scattering length ($a$), and the regime where $a > R$.
main_assumptions: The proof assumes the potentials are real, local, and finite-range, and specifically focuses on the condition $a > R$.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a repulsive potential and its wave function; Figures 2, 3, and 4 illustrate the zero-energy wave function and its asymptotic form, demonstrating the spatial extent of the potential's influence.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the phenomenological problem of $r_0$ in hadron physics, defines the mathematical model of the potential, provides a rigorous proof of the positivity of $r_0$, verifies the result with numerical models (barrier/well and Yukawa), and discusses the implications for multiquark configurations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Repulsive potential. The function 𝑢0 is shown in black, while the dashed line represents the asymptotic expression. The scattering length is positive, as the line intersects the positive 𝑟−axis, and lies between 0 and 𝑅.

FIG. 2. Attractive potential not strong enough to support a bound state. The function 𝑢0 is shown in black, while the dashed line represents the asymptotic expression. The scattering length is negative, as the line intersects the negative 𝑟-axis.

FIG. 3. Attractive potential that supports a shallow bound state. The function 𝑢0 is shown in black, while the dashed line represents the asymptotic expression. The scattering length is positive and 𝑎≫𝑅.

FIG. 4. The wave function 𝑢𝐵of the shallow bound state is shown in black. The spatial extent of the wave function is much larger than the range of the potential. The dashed line represents the exponential behavior of the asymptotic expression.

FIG. 5. Solid line: zero-energy wavefunction for the potential in Eq. (35) with the parameters listed in Tab. I. The dashed line indicates the solution for the purely attractive problem. The dash-dotted line represents the asymptotic solution 1 −¯𝑟/ ¯𝑎, which is common to both solutions since they share the same scattering length. The vertical dotted lines indicate the end of the repulsive region (¯𝑟= ¯𝑟1) and the range of the potential (¯𝑟= 1), respectively.
